intro:
Canaan names both a man — the grandson of Noah — and the land that bore his name, the territory promised to Abraham and his descendants and the geographical stage on which most of the Old Testament unfolds. The two senses are joined in the biblical story: the land is called Canaan because it was settled by the offspring of Canaan son of Ham, whom Israel was later commissioned to dispossess.

As a person, Canaan was the fourth son of Ham and the object of Noah's curse after Ham's shameful conduct toward his father: "Cursed be Canaan; a servant of servants shall he be to his brothers" (Genesis 9:25). From him descended a cluster of peoples — Sidon, Heth, and the Jebusites, Amorites, Girgashites, Hivites, and others (Genesis 10:15–18) — who occupied the land before Israel. As a land, "Canaan" first denoted the Phoenician coastal strip and broadened to mean the whole country between the Jordan and the Mediterranean, from Sidon in the north toward Gaza and the cities of the plain in the south (Genesis 10:19). Its inhabitants are summed up in the recurring list of "the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites" (Exodus 3:8), and the land is famously described as one "flowing with milk and honey."

The covenantal weight of Canaan begins with the call of Abram, who journeys into the land and to whom the LORD declares, "To your offspring I will give this land" (Genesis 12:7); it is formally granted as "an everlasting possession" in Genesis 17:8. The patriarchs live there as resident aliens, burying their dead in its soil as a pledge of future inheritance. The promise is then deferred through four centuries in Egypt — partly, the text explains, because "the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete" (Genesis 15:16) — so that the conquest is at once the gift of grace to Israel and the judgment of God upon entrenched Canaanite wickedness. The whole arc of Exodus, the wilderness, and the spies (Numbers 13:1–2) is oriented toward this land.

Under Joshua, Israel crosses the Jordan and takes possession; the manna ceases once they eat of the land's produce (Joshua 5:12), marking the transition from wilderness sojourn to settled inheritance. Canaan thus becomes the "Promised Land," later called Israel and the Land of Israel. Theologically, the land functions throughout Scripture as the tangible sign of covenant blessing and rest, and the New Testament reaches behind the geography to its deeper meaning: the rest Canaan offered was incomplete (Hebrews 4:8–9), pointing forward to the everlasting "rest" and the heavenly homeland for which the patriarchs ultimately longed (Hebrews 11:16).

The conquest itself displays both judgment and grace. At Jericho the walls fell at Israel's shout, and only Rahab's household was spared (Joshua 6:20; Joshua 6:25); Ai was taken once Achan's sin had been purged (Joshua 8:1); the Gibeonites secured their lives by a ruse (Joshua 9:15); and the coalition of northern kings was shattered at Hazor (Joshua 11:10). When the land was at last apportioned among the tribes, the verdict stood: "Not one word of all the good promises that the LORD had made... had failed" (Joshua 21:45). Yet the rest Israel entered under Joshua was provisional, and it is no accident that Joshua is the same name as Jesus: the true Yeshua leads his people not into a strip of earth but into the everlasting inheritance and Sabbath rest of God (Hebrews 4:8–9), the better country toward which Canaan always pointed.
hitchcock_meaning: merchant; trader; or that humbles and subdues

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Jericho
First city taken; its walls fell at Israel's shout (Josh 6:20)
Ai
Taken after Achan's sin was judged (Josh 8:1)
Gibeon
Whose people made peace by a ruse, then were rescued (Josh 9:15)
Hazor
Head of the northern coalition, burned by Joshua (Josh 11:10)
Lachish
A southern fortress-city captured in the campaign (Josh 10:32)
Gezer
Canaanite city long unsubdued on the coastal plain (Josh 16:10)
Shechem
Where Joshua renewed the covenant with Israel (Josh 24:25)
Hebron
Patriarchal town and Caleb's inheritance (Josh 14:13)
Bethel
House of God where Abraham and Jacob worshipped (Gen 12:8)
Gilgal
First camp west of Jordan; the manna ceased here (Josh 5:10)
Jerusalem (Jebus)
Jebusite stronghold, later the city of David (Josh 15:63)
Beersheba
Southern bound of the land, well of the patriarchs (Gen 21:31)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places appear on the map above.

cells: Abraham
First given the land as an everlasting possession (Gen 17:8)
Melchizedek
King of Salem who blessed Abram in the land (Gen 14:18)
Joshua
Led Israel across Jordan to take the inheritance (Josh 1:2)
Caleb
Faithful spy who received Hebron for his trust (Josh 14:13)
Rahab
Jericho's harlot who believed and was saved (Josh 6:25)
Achan
Whose theft brought defeat until it was judged (Josh 7:1)
